draft.introduction: The healing of the paralytic lowered through the roof. Jesus’ pronouncement “your sins are forgiven” provokes the first christological controversy in Mark: “Who can forgive sins but God alone?”

translation.text: And when he returned to Capernaum after some days, it was reported that he was at home. And many were gathered together, so that there was no more room. And when they could not get near him because of the crowd, they removed the roof above him, and they let down the bed on which the paralytic lay. And when Jesus saw their faith, he said to the paralytic, “Son, your sins are forgiven.”

And immediately Jesus, perceiving in his spirit that they thus questioned within themselves, said to them, “Why do you question these things in your hearts? Which is easier, to say, ‘Your sins are forgiven,’ or to say, ‘Rise, take up your bed and walk’? But that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins”—he said to the paralytic—“I say to you, rise, pick up your bed, and go home.”
translation.original_text: τίς δύναται ἀφιέναι ἁμαρτίας εἰ μὴ εἷς ὁ θεός

translator_notes.note: The scribes’ objection—“Who can forgive sins but God alone?” (τίς δύναται ἀφιέναι ἁμαρτίας εἰ μὴ εἷς ὁ θεός)—is theologically correct. Mark’s narrative logic implies that Jesus’ exercise of this divine prerogative is itself a christological claim.
